clinical_history: This ECG, from an elderly man with chronic heart failure, is relatively unchanged over at least 6 months. What did the echocardiogram show?
diagnosis: The ECG shows borderline resting sinus tachycardia at about 100/min with relatively low limb lead voltage and prominent precordial voltage; an intraventricular conduction delay with a right ventricular component; left atrial abnormality and left ventricular hypertrophy, and extensive pathologic Q waves from V1-V6 with ST elevations in V1-V5, as well as an rSrs morphology in I and aVL. Inverted T waves are present in V5 and V6. The persistence of ST elevations and Q waves weeks to months after an MI suggests the presence of a severe wall motion abnormality, sometimes with frank dyskinesis (aneurysm). The finding of rSr type complexes in the left precordial leads has also been reported in this context (El-Sherif sign). The sinus tachycardia, low limb/prominent precordial voltage and left atrial abnormality, along with the other findings are also consistent with severe ventricular dysfunction. Echocardiogram also showed a possible apical left ventricular thrombus, which may occur with large akinetic or dyskinetic zones.
